figures.caption: Figure 2: A frozen JEPA encoder maps observations to a latent space, where centroid-based assignment selects either a shift-specific expert or the ID reject option.

Figure 5: PCA 2D projection of JEPA embeddings. ID, torso mass 3x, and torso mass 5x states from rollouts of baseline 400k.pt, projected to first two principal components of the 64-dim PCA space used by the routing module. (a) walker-walk: ID centroid µID is well-separated from shift centroids (∥µID − µ3x∥ = 20.6, ∥µID − µ5x∥ = 23.3); however, the two shift centroids are close to each other (∥µ3x − µ5x∥ = 5.0), explaining occasional cross-routing between them. (b) cheetahrun: ID and torso-mass 3× partially overlap (∥µID − µ3x∥ = 6.9), while torso-mass 5× is clearly separated. This motivates the 5x-only routing rule used for cheetah-run experiments (Section 4.3).

Table 4: Cross-task validation on cheetah-run (30 seeds, paired bootstrap). Method uses 5×- only routing due to insufficient ID–3× centroid separability in cheetah JEPA space (ID–3× distance 7.84 vs. ID–5× distance 15.43). Under this routing, torso-mass 5× shows highly significant improvement (p < 0.001, P (improve) = 1.0); torso-mass 3× shows positive but marginally significant trend; ID is preserved.

Figure 1: JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth. The baseline pixel controller produces a base action abaset . A frozen JEPA encoder maps a short observation context to a representation used only for problem indexing, which selects either a shift-specific local expert or the ID reject option. The selected expert predicts a residual correction ∆a (c) t , and the final action is at = abaset +∆a (c) t . Thus, JEPA is used for indexing rather than control, while adaptation is achieved through reusable local residual experts.

Figure 4: Per-seed paired comparison on walker-walk (30 seeds). Each point represents one evaluation seed; coordinates are baseline return (x) and HarderPairs return (y). Points above the y = x diagonal correspond to seeds where HarderPairs outperforms baseline. Annotations show paired ∆, 95% CI, and p-value from 10000-resample paired bootstrap.

Figure 8: OOD subfamilies remain highly overlapping in PCA space. Kernel density estimates on the three PCA dimensions with the largest known-vs.-novel OOD KS statistics show that ID is clearly separated, whereas all five OOD shifts largely overlap. This supports the conclusion that OOD sub-family discrimination is representation-limited rather than algorithmically missing.

Figure 7: Sub-family discrimination is geometrically infeasible in the frozen-JEPA representation. (a) Pairwise cosine similarity between cluster centroids in the 64-dim PCA space. ID is wellseparated from all OOD shifts (similarity ≤ 0.5), but every pair of OOD shifts is near-collinear (similarity 0.95–0.99) on both tasks. (b) Within-cluster spread relative to between-cluster distance, computed against ID centroid (left bar) and against the nearest OOD centroid (right bar). Ratios above 1× (dashed line) indicate the discrimination boundary. ID-vs-OOD ratios remain below 1×, while OOD-vs-OOD ratios reach 8.1× on walker and 4.3× on cheetah, indicating that OOD subfamilies are not separable along any centroid-based decision rule.

Figure 6: Auto ID-rejection achieves near-perfect discrimination across both tasks. ROC curves for the frozen-JEPA + simple density detector on (a) walker-walk and (b) cheetah-run. Each panel shows discrimination between in-distribution windows and four OOD subsets: known-shift 3×, knownshift 5×, novel-shift family (4×, 6×, 8× merged), and all OOD combined. The selected operating threshold τ (5% in-distribution false-positive rate) is marked with a star. The detector achieves AUC ≥ 0.99 on both tasks and generalizes to novel shifts unseen during calibration, supporting autorejection as a plug-and-play replacement for the manual centroid threshold used in the main routing rule.

Figure 3: Per-step cumulative return on walker-walk torso-mass shifts. Mean cumulative return over 30 evaluation seeds for the pixel baseline and HarderPairs. Shaded bands show ±1 std. HarderPairs shows a consistent advantage on both torso-mass 3× and torso-mass 5×. Statistical results are reported in Table 1.
